storylines.label: gcam / SSP2-Base
storylines.description: SSP2, a middle-of-the-road development pathway, combined with a baseline case without additional tightening beyond the model setup implied by the file name.
storylines.scope_note: This GCAM extract reports global population, GDP, temperature, CO2, electricity output, DAC, and covered steel/cement final energy, but not a clean economy-wide final-energy or aggregate GHG series.

sections.title: Scenario Setup
sections.content: SSP2, a middle-of-the-road development pathway, combined with a baseline case without additional tightening beyond the model setup implied by the file name.

sections.title: Interpretive Reading
sections.content: This is structurally closer to a weak-policy pathway, so economic and energy growth face less climate constraint. By 2100 this remains a relatively warm pathway, which usually means mitigation is weaker, later, or both. Because CO2 stays positive through 2100 and carbon removal remains limited, the transition stops short of a full net-zero-style outcome.

sections.title: Population and Economy
sections.content: Starting from 7.74 billion people in 2020, population reaches 9.13 billion people by 2100; the 2050 value is 9.31 billion people. The biggest absolute population increases to mid-century are in Africa Western, India, and Africa Eastern. Starting from $116.8 trillion in 2020, GDP reaches $597.1 trillion by 2100; the 2050 value is $261.3 trillion. By 2050 the largest GDP blocks in the file are China, India, and United States.

sections.title: Climate and Emissions
sections.content: Starting from 1.31C in 2020, global mean temperature reaches 3.53C by 2100; the 2050 value is 2.16C. Starting from 10.70 Gt/yr in 2020, CO2 emissions reach 15.80 Gt/yr by 2100; the 2050 value is 13.16 Gt/yr. The largest regional CO2 sources around 2050 are China, India, and United States.

sections.title: Energy System
sections.content: The file does not include a clean economy-wide final-energy total, but it does report steel and cement final energy. Starting from 50.1 EJ/yr in 2020, that covered industrial energy falls to 28.0 EJ/yr by 2100; the 2050 value is 36.9 EJ/yr. Starting from 100.9 EJ/yr in 2020, electricity output reaches 396.5 EJ/yr by 2100; the 2050 value is 241.5 EJ/yr. Electrification is visible in the end-use split: covered steel-and-cement electricity share moves from 10% in 2020 to 18% in 2100.

sections.title: Carbon Management and Biomass
sections.content: Starting from 0 Mt/yr in 2020, direct air capture stays broadly flat and is still around 0 Mt/yr by 2100; the 2050 value is 0 Mt/yr.

sections.title: Regional Pattern
sections.content: Population growth to mid-century is concentrated in Africa Western, India, and Africa Eastern. Mid-century economic weight is concentrated in China, India, and United States. The heaviest CO2-emitting regions around 2050 are China, India, and United States.

sections.title: Scenario Setup
sections.content: SSP2, a middle-of-the-road development pathway, combined with a low-emissions variant.

sections.title: Interpretive Reading
sections.content: This sits on the low-emissions side of its model family, so the storyline is about accelerated transition rather than simple fossil continuation. This is best read as an overshoot pathway: warming peaks first, and only later does the system work temperatures back down. The route to low warming is not purely about lower demand or cleaner electricity: the model leans heavily on carbon management and late-century net-negative emissions.

sections.title: Interpretive Reading
sections.content: This sits on the high-emissions side of its model family, so the transition remains incomplete or delayed relative to lower-emissions variants. By 2100 this remains a relatively warm pathway, which usually means mitigation is weaker, later, or both. Because CO2 stays positive through 2100 and carbon removal remains limited, the transition stops short of a full net-zero-style outcome.
